narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like choosing full-time study and your solid LSAT score. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try—this high risk led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school grade decile, which was in the bottom range compared to typical students, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 grade decile, also at the lower end relative to averages, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pulling the outlook downward. While your LSAT score of 35 was a bit below the average of about 37, it actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward bar passage success. Your undergraduate GPA around 2.9, lower than the typical 3.2, offered a small counterbalance in the positive direction, and your middle family income bracket had a minor influence toward higher risk. Being full-time, like most applicants, provided just a tiny boost toward passing. Overall, your early and later undergrad performance stood out as weaker than what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar, unlike your LSAT which held up better against the norm. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes these patterns from historical data. To move forward, focus on boosting your grades through targeted tutoring or additional coursework to climb those deciles, retake the LSAT if possible to push closer to or above average, and consider programs offering bar prep support for underrepresented students like you. Reapplying after strengthening these areas could shift the prediction in your favor, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing—you've got this.
